sanskrit_devanagari:
आवृतं ज्ञानमेतेन ज्ञानिनो नित्यवैरिणा ।
कामरूपेण कौन्तेय दुष्पूरेणानलेन च ॥ ३९ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
āvṛtaṁ jñānam etena
jñānino nitya-vairiṇā |
kāma-rūpeṇa kaunteya
duṣpūreṇānalena ca || 39 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence as published)

exact (academic critical edition; cross-reference Bhg_03.039 = MBh_06,025.039). Body text identical; word-segmentation differs by edition.

translations.verbatim_quote: Thus the wise living entity’s pure consciousness becomes covered by his eternal enemy in the form of lust, which is never satisfied and which burns like fire.
(See holy-bhagavad-gita.org for full Mukundananda rendering; agreement with Prabhupada noted in disagreements section where it diverges materially.)

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: It is said in the Manu-smṛti that lust cannot be satisfied by any amount of sense enjoyment, just as fire is never extinguished by a constant supply of fuel. In the material world, the center of all activities is sex, and thus this material world is called maithunya-āgāra, or the shackles of sex life.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.39 Jnanam, Knowledge; is avrtam, covered; etena, by this; nityavairina, constant enemy; jnaninah, of the wise. For the wise person knows even earlier, ‘I am being induced by this into evil.’ And he always [Both at the time when desire arises in him, and also when he is forced to
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara: āvṛtam jñānam etena — knowledge is covered by this. jñāninaḥ nitya-vairiṇā — by the constant enemy of the wise. kāma-rūpeṇa kaunteya — in the form of kāma, O son of Kunti. duṣpūreṇa analena ca — insatiable, like fire. The verse extends 3.38: kāma is structurally permanent (nitya-vairin), and feeding it (the obvious remedy) does not exhaust it (duṣpūreṇa).

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.39 The knowledge, having the self for its subject, of this embodied person (the Jiva) whose nature is knowledge, is enveloped by this constant enemy in the shape of desire, which brings about attachment for sense-objects.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja: kāma is the jñānin's nitya-vairin — perennial enemy. The threat persists even after knowledge has been gained; jñāna alone does not eliminate kāma. duṣpūreṇa anala — insatiable, like fire — establishes that the wrong remedy (yielding to satisfy) is doubly wrong: kāma cannot be satisfied, and yielding inflames it further.

positions.rendering: thus the wise living entity's pure consciousness becomes covered by his eternal enemy in the form of lust, which is never satisfied and which burns like fire
the knowledge of even the most discerning gets covered by this constant enemy in the form of insatiable desire, which is never satisfied and burns like fire

nitya-vairin — the constant, perennial enemy of the wise; the wise are precisely those who recognise the enemy as such; duṣpūreṇa — insatiable; analena — like fire, which consumes its fuel without diminishing its hunger

kāma is the wise person's eternal enemy because, even after the seeker has gained knowledge, the threat does not vanish; duṣpūreṇa anala — insatiable like fire — names the futility of attempting to satisfy kāma by feeding it

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: Both commentators emphasise that nitya-vairiṇā implies the threat is structural and ongoing — the wise have not transcended it merely by knowing of it. duṣpūreṇa anala — insatiable like fire — closes the obvious wrong remedy: feeding kāma does not satisfy it. Translators capture both but sometimes obscure the structural-permanence claim.

literal_meaning: By this constant enemy of the wise, in the form of kāma, knowledge is covered, O son of Kunti — insatiable, like fire.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Continuation of 3.38's covering simile, with two new claims: (1) kāma is the wise person's nitya-vairin — constant enemy — meaning the threat does not vanish at the level of knowledge alone; (2) it is duṣpūreṇa — insatiable, like fire — which means feeding it does not satisfy it. Both claims sharpen the diagnosis: the enemy persists despite knowledge, and yielding to it does not exhaust it.
